generic_phrases: change one thing at a time

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshooting DNS on Windows with PowerShell
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Diagnose Windows DNS and network configuration problems with safe PowerShell commands.

evergreen_topics.title: How to Audit GitHub Actions Token Permissions
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Audit and minimize GITHUB_TOKEN permissions in GitHub Actions workflows.

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshooting Windows Event Logs with PowerShell
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Query and filter Windows Event Logs efficiently with safe PowerShell and command-line examples.
generation_constraints: Prefer read-only Get-WinEvent and wevtutil query examples. Do not include commands that clear or delete event logs unless the command is omitted and the risk is explained.

evergreen_topics.title: Common Kubernetes Probe Misconfigurations and Fixes
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Diagnose and correct liveness, readiness, and startup probe settings that cause restarts or unavailable workloads.
generation_constraints: Use version-neutral probe fields from current Kubernetes documentation and explain the operational difference between liveness, readiness, and startup probes.

seed_sources.title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshooting Kubernetes RBAC with kubectl auth can-i
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Diagnose Kubernetes authorization failures using RBAC inspection and kubectl auth can-i.
generation_constraints: For kubectl impersonation, state only that the caller must be allowed to use impersonation for the --as option. Do not describe vague privileged users or infer broader permissions.

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshoot Microsoft Entra Sign-in Errors with Sign-in Diagnostics
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Diagnose Microsoft Entra sign-in failures using sign-in logs and Sign-in Diagnostics before changing Conditional Access or authentication settings.

seed_sources.title: What are Microsoft Entra sign-in logs?

offline_fallback.description: A safe Microsoft Entra sign-in troubleshooting workflow that uses sign-in logs and Sign-in Diagnostics to isolate the failure before changing authentication or access policies.
offline_fallback.overview: Microsoft Entra sign-in troubleshooting should begin with the affected sign-in record and its diagnostic details. This isolates whether the failure is associated with the account, application, device, network, authentication method, or an access policy before an administrator changes Conditional Access or user settings.
offline_fallback.preparation: Ask the affected user for the approximate time, application, device, network location, and the exact error shown. Confirm the administrator is authorized to view the relevant tenant data and use an appropriately scoped account. Preserve the original sign-in details before testing with another user or another device.

steps.title: Find the matching sign-in
steps.body: Filter the sign-in records by the user, application, and the smallest time window that contains the failure. Confirm that the record matches the reported attempt rather than a background token refresh or a different application.
steps.source_guidance: Use the sign-in-log overview to confirm which records and fields are relevant.

steps.title: Read the diagnostic result before editing policy
steps.body: Open the diagnostic information for the selected record and note the result, failure code, and any policy or authentication context shown. Keep the record identifier with the ticket so the same event can be reviewed after a controlled test.
steps.source_guidance: Use Sign-in Diagnostics for the supported diagnostic workflow and interpretation.

steps.title: Validate the affected condition with a narrow test
steps.body: Test only the suspected boundary, such as the application assignment, authentication method, device state, or named network condition. Use a non-production test account when policy allows, and do not disable a broad protection merely to prove that a sign-in can succeed.
steps.source_guidance: Use the activity-detail reference to check the exact fields reported for the sign-in.

symptoms.symptom: One user cannot access one application
symptoms.likely_cause: User, application, or assignment-specific condition
symptoms.next_check: Compare the selected sign-in record with a successful user in the same application.

symptoms.symptom: Sign-in fails only from one device or network
symptoms.likely_cause: Device posture, client, or network condition
symptoms.next_check: Confirm the recorded client and location context before changing policy.

symptoms.symptom: A policy-related error appears
symptoms.likely_cause: A requirement was not satisfied
symptoms.next_check: Review the sign-in diagnostic details and make one approved, narrow test.

checklist: Capture the exact time, user, application, device, and user-facing error.
Locate the matching sign-in record and preserve its identifier.
Review diagnostic and activity details before changing an authentication or access policy.
Test one suspected condition with an approved, reversible change.
Record the result and remove any temporary exception at its agreed expiry time.
offline_fallback.version_context: Microsoft Entra monitoring and health documentation checked at publication time.

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshoot Windows Scheduled Tasks with PowerShell
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Diagnose Windows scheduled task failures with read-only PowerShell and schtasks queries before changing task credentials, triggers, or actions.

offline_fallback.description: A read-only Windows Scheduled Tasks troubleshooting workflow using PowerShell and schtasks to inspect task definitions, state, and recent execution results safely.
offline_fallback.overview: A scheduled task failure is easiest to diagnose by separating the task definition, its current state, and the result of its most recent run. Read the existing configuration and history first so an administrator does not overwrite the evidence by changing the trigger, action, credentials, or working directory too early.
offline_fallback.preparation: Identify the task name, host, expected schedule, last known successful run, and the account or service context approved to inspect it. Gather the task path if the environment has folders with duplicate display names. Do not run, disable, export secrets from, or re-register the task while collecting the first evidence set.

steps.title: Inspect the task definition
steps.body: Use a read-only task query to confirm the task path, action, trigger, principal, and settings that are actually registered on the target host. Compare the live definition with the approved configuration rather than relying on a local copy of a script.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Get-ScheduledTask reference for the supported task-definition query.

steps.title: Check state and last run information
steps.body: Read the task information associated with the same registered task and record the current state, last run time, and last task result shown by the system. Correlate the timestamp with application and system evidence before deciding that the scheduler itself is the failing component.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Get-ScheduledTaskInfo reference for the task-information fields.

steps.title: Use a command-line view only when needed
steps.body: In a recovery console or an existing command-line workflow, use the documented schtasks query view to compare the task's registered state. Keep the query read-only and avoid changing run-as credentials or trigger settings until the evidence identifies the specific boundary.
steps.source_guidance: Use the schtasks reference for its documented query and administrative behavior.

symptoms.symptom: Task is present but never runs
symptoms.likely_cause: Trigger, conditions, or scheduler state
symptoms.next_check: Compare the registered trigger and state with the expected schedule.

symptoms.symptom: Task runs but the action fails
symptoms.likely_cause: Action path, working context, dependency, or permissions
symptoms.next_check: Correlate the last run result with the application and system logs.

symptoms.symptom: Task works manually but not on schedule
symptoms.likely_cause: Trigger or execution context differs
symptoms.next_check: Compare the scheduled context with the approved manual test context.

checklist: Record the host, task path, expected schedule, and last known good run.
Inspect the registered task definition before changing it.
Capture current state and last-run information from the same task.
Correlate the time of failure with application and Windows event evidence.
Make one approved, reversible change and verify the next scheduled execution.
offline_fallback.version_context: Windows Server and PowerShell ScheduledTasks documentation checked at publication time.

evergreen_topics.title: Investigate Windows Firewall Traffic with PowerShell
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Trace blocked Windows Firewall traffic by mapping the active profile, a narrow rule, and its port filter before changing policy.

offline_fallback.description: A read-only PowerShell workflow for diagnosing Windows Firewall profile, rule, and port-filter problems before modifying a rule or exposing a service.
offline_fallback.overview: Windows Firewall troubleshooting should distinguish the active profile, the rule that may apply, and the port or protocol being tested. Inspect those boundaries with read-only queries before creating an exception, disabling a profile, or assuming that the application is listening on the expected address.
offline_fallback.preparation: Record the host, active network connection, application or service, direction of the traffic, protocol, port, remote endpoint, and the time of the failure. Confirm the investigation is authorized and avoid disabling a firewall profile as a diagnostic shortcut. Preserve the observed state so a later change can be compared with the original configuration.

steps.title: Identify the active firewall profile
steps.body: Inspect the configured firewall profiles on the affected host and note the profile that applies to the network connection in use. Keep the result with the incident record before comparing rules or testing the service again.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Get-NetFirewallProfile reference for supported profile inspection.

steps.title: Find the relevant rule without changing it
steps.body: Query the existing firewall rules and narrow the result by the known application, display name, direction, or enabled state. Confirm that a rule is associated with the expected profile before interpreting its presence as evidence that the traffic is allowed.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Get-NetFirewallRule reference for supported rule inspection.

steps.title: Check the port and protocol boundary
steps.body: Inspect the port-filter information for the candidate rule and compare it with the actual protocol and port used by the affected service. Correlate this with a separate connectivity or service-level test rather than opening a broad range of ports.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Get-NetFirewallPortFilter reference for the documented port-filter view.

symptoms.symptom: Service is reachable on one network but not another
symptoms.likely_cause: Different active profile or network path
symptoms.next_check: Compare the active profile and rule applicability on both paths.

symptoms.symptom: A rule exists but the connection still fails
symptoms.likely_cause: Direction, profile, port, protocol, or service mismatch
symptoms.next_check: Inspect the rule and port filter, then test the service separately.

symptoms.symptom: Opening a port appears to fix the issue
symptoms.likely_cause: The original boundary is not yet understood
symptoms.next_check: Revert the broad test and identify the smallest approved rule change.

evidence_queries.title: Record the active connection profile
evidence_queries.command: Get-NetConnectionProfile | Select-Object Name, NetworkCategory, InterfaceAlias, IPv4Connectivity, IPv6Connectivity
evidence_queries.explanation: This captures the connection context that determines which firewall profile applies. Keep the interface alias with the failed-flow record rather than assuming the profile from the network name alone.

evidence_queries.title: Inspect profile policy
evidence_queries.command: Get-NetFirewallProfile | Select-Object Name, Enabled, DefaultInboundAction, DefaultOutboundAction
evidence_queries.explanation: This shows the policy boundary for each Windows Firewall profile. Compare the active profile with the expected inbound and outbound defaults before narrowing the investigation to an individual rule.

evidence_queries.title: Locate enabled candidate rules
evidence_queries.command: Get-NetFirewallRule -Enabled True | Select-Object DisplayName, Direction, Action, Profile, PolicyStoreSource
evidence_queries.explanation: Use known application, service, or display-name terms to reduce the candidate set. The result is evidence of a configured rule, not evidence that the rule matches the tested protocol and port.

evidence_queries.title: Inspect the candidate port filter
evidence_queries.command: Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName '<rule name>' | Get-NetFirewallPortFilter | Select-Object Protocol, LocalPort, RemotePort
evidence_queries.explanation: Run this only after identifying a specific candidate rule. Compare its protocol and port values with the observed flow and retain the original rule name in the incident record.

operational_notes: Start with a single failed flow, not a general statement that the application is blocked. Name the initiating host, destination host, direction, protocol, local port, remote port, and the active network category. A firewall rule can be present yet irrelevant because the tested flow has the wrong direction, belongs to another profile, or does not match the port filter. This record gives the rule query a precise target.

Profile selection is a separate question from rule discovery. A domain, private, or public profile can have different defaults and different rule applicability. Capture the profile settings that apply at the time of the failure, then determine whether the candidate rule is enabled for that profile. Do not infer profile applicability from a rule display name or from a successful test made while connected through another network.

A rule is only one layer of the packet decision. Its direction, action, program or service scope, address constraints, protocol, and port filter must agree with the flow under investigation. Compare those attributes one by one with the observed connection. If a rule permits inbound TCP traffic on a different port, it is not evidence that the tested outbound UDP flow should succeed.

Use a deliberately narrow remediation only after the observed flow and the applicable rule boundary match. Avoid disabling a profile or creating an any-to-any exception to prove a hypothesis; those shortcuts replace useful evidence with a larger exposure. If a temporary rule is authorized, document its scope and expiry, retest the original flow, and remove the test rule when the investigation is complete.
checklist: Record the service, traffic direction, protocol, port, and active network context.
Inspect active firewall profiles before changing a rule.
Query the candidate rule and verify its enabled state and profile scope.
Compare the rule's port-filter information with the service actually being tested.
Apply and document only the narrowest approved, reversible remediation.
offline_fallback.version_context: Windows Server NetSecurity PowerShell documentation checked at publication time.

evergreen_topics.title: Diagnose Windows Service Failures with PowerShell
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Identify the Windows service state, registered configuration, and dependency boundary before changing startup, recovery, or service-account settings.

offline_fallback.description: A read-only PowerShell workflow for diagnosing Windows service state, configuration, and query results before changing startup, recovery, or service-account settings.
offline_fallback.overview: Windows service troubleshooting is safer when the current service state, its registered configuration, and the result of the most recent failure are kept separate. Start with read-only inspection so a restart, startup-type change, or service-account edit does not destroy the evidence needed to identify the real boundary.
offline_fallback.preparation: Record the host, service name, display name, expected behavior, first observed failure time, and the account or workload affected. Confirm whether the service is local or remote and use an approved account for the inspection. Do not restart, reconfigure, or change credentials during the first evidence-collection pass.

steps.title: Identify the service and its current state
steps.body: Use a read-only service query to distinguish the service name from its display name and capture its current state. Compare the live result with the expected state and record any dependent service or application that is reporting the failure.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Get-Service reference for supported service discovery and state inspection.

steps.title: Inspect the registered service information
steps.body: Use a read-only CIM query to examine the service information relevant to the incident, then compare the observed configuration with the approved service design. Keep the result tied to the same host and timestamp as the initial state query.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Get-CimInstance reference for supported CIM instance inspection.

steps.title: Confirm the command-line query view when required
steps.body: In a recovery console or an established command-line runbook, use the documented sc.exe query behavior to compare the registered service result. Treat the query as evidence collection and correlate it with service-specific and Windows event information before applying a fix.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Sc.exe query reference for its documented query syntax and output behavior.

symptoms.symptom: Service is stopped unexpectedly
symptoms.likely_cause: Service failure, dependency, startup condition, or host problem
symptoms.next_check: Capture the current state and correlate the failure time with related evidence.

symptoms.symptom: Service starts then stops
symptoms.likely_cause: Application, dependency, account, or configuration boundary
symptoms.next_check: Inspect the registered service information before attempting another start.

symptoms.symptom: Service behaves differently across hosts
symptoms.likely_cause: Configuration or environment drift
symptoms.next_check: Compare the same read-only service and CIM results from both hosts.

evidence_queries.title: Resolve the registered service identity
evidence_queries.command: Get-Service -Name '<service name>' | Select-Object Name, DisplayName, Status, StartType
evidence_queries.explanation: Use the registered service name from the incident or map it from the display name first. Record the state before trying a restart so that the initial failure remains observable.

evidence_queries.title: Inspect the service CIM record
evidence_queries.command: Get-CimInstance Win32_Service -Filter "Name='<service name>'" | Select-Object Name, State, StartMode, StartName, PathName
evidence_queries.explanation: This separates the service's registered configuration from its current status. Compare the returned account and executable context with the approved design for the affected host.

evidence_queries.title: Capture the command-line state
evidence_queries.explanation: Use the command-line query where it is part of the established runbook or recovery environment. Retain the output with the timestamp and service name so it can be correlated with other evidence.

operational_notes: Treat the display name and service name as separate identifiers. Incident reports often contain a friendly display name while command-line and CIM results use the registered service name. Resolve that mapping before comparing hosts or looking for dependencies. A query against a similarly named service can produce plausible output while leaving the failing workload unexplained.

State alone does not explain a service failure. Record whether the service is stopped, start-pending, running, or repeatedly returning to a stopped state, together with the first observed failure time. That timing lets you correlate the service result with application logs, system events, updates, certificate changes, or dependency failures instead of assuming that a restart is a diagnosis.

Registered service configuration provides a different kind of evidence from live state. Compare the relevant configuration fields and the account or executable context with the approved design, especially when the same service behaves differently on another host. Keep environment drift, account permissions, and dependency availability as distinct hypotheses until the evidence connects them to the failed start or workload.

If a remediation is needed, change one boundary at a time and preserve the before-and-after result. A startup-type change, recovery edit, credential change, or restart can have operational effects beyond the immediate symptom. Use the smallest approved action, verify the workload that originally failed, and document any rollback condition so that a temporary recovery does not conceal the underlying cause. Include the exact service name, host, start mode, and account context in the final handoff so another operator can reproduce the comparison without relying on memory.
checklist: Record the exact service name, host, expected state, and first observed failure time.
Inspect the current service state without restarting it.
Capture the relevant registered service information with a read-only query.
Correlate the result with application and Windows event evidence.
Apply one approved, reversible change only after identifying the failing boundary.
offline_fallback.version_context: Windows Server and PowerShell service-management documentation checked at publication time.

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshoot Windows Time Synchronization with w32tm
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Diagnose Windows Time service source, status, peer, and resynchronization failures with bounded w32tm queries before changing domain time configuration.

seed_sources.title: Windows Time service tools and settings
How the Windows Time service works
w32tm resync reports no time data available

offline_fallback.description: A read-first Windows Time service troubleshooting workflow that uses w32tm status, source, peer, and bounded offset queries before changing NTP or domain hierarchy settings.
offline_fallback.overview: Windows time failures should be separated into the configured source, the source that is currently selected, peer reachability, and the measured offset. Read those boundaries first because changing peers or forcing a resynchronization too early can hide whether the problem is policy, domain hierarchy, name resolution, UDP 123 reachability, or an unavailable source.
offline_fallback.preparation: Record the affected host, domain membership, operating-system version, current time and time zone, authentication symptom, and approximate offset from a trusted reference. Use an approved administrative console for w32tm, preserve the first query results, and do not edit registry values or domain-controller time policy during the initial evidence pass.

steps.title: Read the current source and status
steps.body: Query the current time source and detailed service status on the affected host. Keep the source name, stratum, last successful synchronization time, and reported offset together so the evidence describes one observation rather than several unrelated commands.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Windows Time tools reference for documented w32tm query behavior and required context.

steps.title: Compare peers with the domain design
steps.body: Inspect the configured peers and determine whether a domain member is expected to follow the domain hierarchy or a reviewed manual peer list. Compare the runtime result with Group Policy and role expectations before treating a registry value as the active configuration.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Windows Time service architecture reference to interpret hierarchy and source selection.

steps.title: Investigate a no-time-data result
steps.body: When resynchronization reports that no time data is available, verify name resolution, the selected source, network reachability, and relevant service events before repeating the request. A repeated resync is not a substitute for finding why no acceptable sample reached the client.
steps.source_guidance: Use Microsoft's no-time-data troubleshooting article for the documented failure boundaries.

symptoms.symptom: The source is Local CMOS Clock
symptoms.likely_cause: No acceptable upstream source is currently selected
symptoms.next_check: Compare runtime source and peers with the expected domain or manual configuration.

symptoms.symptom: Resync reports no time data
symptoms.likely_cause: Source discovery, DNS, reachability, policy, or peer response problem
symptoms.next_check: Preserve status and peer output, then verify the selected source path.

symptoms.symptom: Only one host has a large offset
symptoms.likely_cause: Host-specific service, policy, network, or virtualization boundary
symptoms.next_check: Compare the same bounded queries with a healthy host in the same role.

checklist: Record host role, domain membership, time zone, observed offset, and failure time.
Capture current source, status, and peer information before changing configuration.
Compare runtime behavior with the intended domain hierarchy or approved manual source.
Verify DNS and the network path to the selected source when samples are unavailable.
Apply one approved change, then repeat the original bounded measurement and retain the before-and-after evidence.
offline_fallback.version_context: Windows Server 2016 through Windows Server 2025 Windows Time service documentation checked at publication time.

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshoot Docker Container Health Checks
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Diagnose Docker containers that remain starting or become unhealthy by inspecting HEALTHCHECK configuration, status, and recorded probe output.

offline_fallback.description: A practical Docker health-check troubleshooting workflow for inspecting the image check, container health state, and recorded probe output before rebuilding or restarting a workload.
offline_fallback.overview: An unhealthy Docker container can still have a running main process. Diagnosis should therefore keep process state, health-check configuration, individual probe results, and application readiness separate. Inspect those facts before restarting the container or weakening the check, because a restart can erase the timing pattern without correcting the command, dependency, or startup budget.
offline_fallback.preparation: Record the container name or ID, image reference, deployment time, expected endpoint or command, current container status, and the first unhealthy timestamp. Use read-only Docker commands against the correct daemon context and preserve the initial inspect output before rebuilding the image, recreating the container, or disabling its health check.

steps.title: Confirm the configured HEALTHCHECK
steps.body: Inspect the image and container configuration to identify the exact health command, interval, timeout, retries, and start period. Distinguish an inherited image check from an override so the remediation is applied to the configuration that actually created the container.
steps.source_guidance: Use the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK reference for instruction forms, timing options, exit status, and stored output behavior.

steps.title: Read the health history
steps.body: Use docker inspect to capture the current health status and recent probe records, including timestamps, exit codes, and bounded output. Compare the probe time with application logs and dependency availability instead of assuming that every nonzero exit is an application crash.
steps.source_guidance: Use the docker inspect reference for supported low-level object inspection and formatted output.

steps.title: Find the affected set without changing it
steps.body: Filter the container list by health state to determine whether the problem is isolated to one instance or shared by containers from the same image or environment. Keep health state separate from running or exited state when deciding the next evidence check.
steps.source_guidance: Use the docker container ls reference for documented health filtering.

symptoms.symptom: Container remains in starting state
symptoms.likely_cause: Start period, slow dependency, or probe execution boundary
symptoms.next_check: Compare probe timestamps and command duration with the application startup timeline.

symptoms.symptom: Container is running but unhealthy
symptoms.likely_cause: Probe command, endpoint, dependency, permission, or timeout failure
symptoms.next_check: Inspect recent probe exit codes and output, then correlate the same timestamp with application logs.

symptoms.symptom: Every replica becomes unhealthy together
symptoms.likely_cause: Shared image, configuration, or external dependency
symptoms.next_check: Compare image identity and probe output across replicas before recreating them.

checklist: Record container, image, daemon context, deployment time, and first unhealthy timestamp.
Inspect the effective health-check command and timing options.
Capture recent health records with exit codes and bounded output.
Correlate probe time with application and dependency evidence.
Change one reviewed boundary, recreate only when required, and verify the original health condition.
offline_fallback.version_context: Current Dockerfile and Docker CLI references checked at publication time.

evergreen_topics.title: Troubleshoot Microsoft Entra Conditional Access with What If
evergreen_topics.search_intent: Use the Microsoft Entra Conditional Access What If evaluation and matching sign-in evidence to explain why a policy applies or does not apply before editing it.

seed_sources.title: Troubleshoot sign-in problems with Conditional Access

offline_fallback.description: A safe Microsoft Entra Conditional Access troubleshooting workflow that compares a complete What If simulation with the matching sign-in record before changing policy assignments or controls.
offline_fallback.overview: Conditional Access troubleshooting requires both a modeled policy evaluation and evidence from the real sign-in. The What If tool explains how enabled and report-only policies evaluate the supplied identity, resource, and conditions, while the sign-in record shows the application, resource, client, device, location, risk, and policy result that actually occurred. Neither should be treated as a universal policy test on its own.
offline_fallback.preparation: Capture the affected identity, target application and resource, sign-in time, request or correlation identifier, client app, device platform, location context, and user-facing error. Use an appropriately scoped read-only role where possible, preserve the original policy state, and do not exclude a broad group or disable a policy merely to confirm that access can succeed.

steps.title: Reproduce the intended conditions in What If
steps.body: Supply the identity, exact target resource, device platform, client app, and every condition that matters to the suspected policy. Review both applying and nonapplying policies and retain the first unmet condition reported for a policy that does not apply.
steps.source_guidance: Use the What If reference for required inputs, evaluation results, and documented limitations such as service dependencies.

steps.title: Match the simulation to the real sign-in
steps.body: Find the sign-in record for the reported attempt and compare its resource, client, device, location, risk, and Conditional Access details with the values used in the simulation. A simulation with missing or different conditions does not explain the recorded event.
steps.source_guidance: Use Microsoft's Conditional Access troubleshooting reference for sign-in-event correlation and policy details.

steps.title: Use diagnostics before narrowing a policy
steps.body: Open the sign-in diagnostic for the matching event and record the recommendation and relevant authentication or policy context. If a change is justified, adjust only the identified assignment or condition through the approved process and retest the same scenario.
steps.source_guidance: Use the sign-in diagnostics reference for the supported diagnostic workflow and its scope.

symptoms.symptom: What If says a policy does not apply but the sign-in was blocked
symptoms.likely_cause: Simulation inputs differ from the recorded resource or conditions
symptoms.next_check: Compare every supplied What If parameter with the matching sign-in fields.

symptoms.symptom: A policy applies unexpectedly
symptoms.likely_cause: Broad assignment, resource dependency, audience, or condition match
symptoms.next_check: Read the applying-policy details and the actual resource or audience in the sign-in record.

symptoms.symptom: One user or device behaves differently
symptoms.likely_cause: Identity, group, device, client, risk, or location-specific condition
symptoms.next_check: Run a complete simulation for each scenario and compare the corresponding sign-in evidence.

checklist: Capture the exact identity, application, resource, client, device, location, and sign-in time.
Run What If with all conditions required by the policies in scope.
Compare applying and nonapplying results with the matching sign-in record.
Review Sign-in Diagnostics before changing an assignment or control.
Apply one approved narrow change, retest the same scenario, and remove any temporary exception.
offline_fallback.version_context: Microsoft Entra Conditional Access What If, troubleshooting, and Sign-in Diagnostics documentation checked at publication time.
